paragraphs.body: Some things to note: I changed the character named Michelle in the last draft to be Naomi. The first few paragraphs were also at the end of my last excerpt, but I slightly changed them and also just kept them in here for context.

annotations.comment: Good idea.

paragraphs.body: When Sam’s dad was still alive, Lynn was much more tolerable. Even likeable, sometimes. She had always had her vices, but would at least take her cigarettes outside and try to be subtle when coming home drunk. Sam’s dad had cared about her, that much she knew. They had grown up together, not far from the rural town Lynn runs the gas station out of, and when Sam’s mother passed away when she was 10, Lynn was there at the wake.

annotations.comment: These are such small attempts at decency. Really nice.

annotations.text: the rural town Lynn runs the gas station out of
annotations.comment: Worth naming this town.

paragraphs.body: “Lynn and I, we have a connection that grew when I wasn’t expecting it to,” Sam’s dad had explained when telling her that he had a fiancée just nine months after the wake, “I love her, Sammy, and your mom wouldn’t have wanted me to be alone.”

And what was she supposed to say to that? So, she smiled and reassured and watched it all happen: their years of long distance, their hushed arguments over Lynn driving to Charlotte every other weekend without any reciprocation, their phone calls with realtors for the tiny yellow house. Sam had no other family, no say in their decisions that would uproot her and what she knew.

So, she smiled and said, “I want you to be happy, Dad,” pinching her own skin so hard to fight the tears that were burning behind her eyes. Sam didn’t say what she really wanted to because she didn’t want to sound like a little kid, but she just couldn’t understand how he would be alone without Lynn…what about her?

Only recently, Sam began to realize the contempt she felt towards Lynn, the resentment towards her father. Her anger at the loneliness their arrangement had deserted her to cope with on her own. The confusion she would always be left to contemplate: why hadn’t she been enough?

annotations.comment: I felt that this paragraph wasn’t really necessary. I got this from the scene.

paragraphs.body: A few months after her dad died, Sam started running. At first, it was a way of coping. She couldn’t think about her loss or feel the ache in her heart when she was running, her blood pumping through her veins and pounding into her ears. After a while, she started to become good at it. Her pace was getting quicker and quicker, and she could run even faster if she put all her effort into short bursts. She joined the track team at her high school the next spring, the only club she was ever a part of. High school had always felt useless to her. Sam wasn’t going to college, a truth she decided upon years ago, so the boring classes and regimented schedule drove her crazy. She knew what she wanted to do, and her countdown to the day she could skip town occupied more space in her head than math or science ever could. Her graduation a few weeks ago managed to rank as one of the best days of her life.

annotations.comment: I wonder if there’s a way to word this that doesn’t lead me to think Sam started running…away?
annotations.context_right: At first, it was a w

annotations.text: knew what she wanted to do,
annotations.comment: I was sort of surprised at this. She does?I think your interesting section on running got swallowed up by the hatred of high school. I wanted to know a little bit more about that before we moved on. Do the other girls on the team like her?

paragraphs.body: There was the one class, though, that she felt like she learned enough from to make up for all the other ones she didn’t care about. Her coach taught it, and suggested Sam take it since she needed an elective to fill in her schedule. Script Writing. It was there that Sam learned about writing for TV, the basics of screenplays and genres and formatting. She loved it. It was for that class that she started seriously perfecting her show, rewriting the pilot episode for her first assignment. Her teacher told her it was the best he had ever read from a student her age, and she would stay after class to get his advice almost every week.

annotations.comment: This seems so specific for a high school! Especially a small school.
This implies that she was already developing the show which, again, seems a little surprising for a kid from a rural town.

paragraphs.body: The show takes place in a fictional California coastal town, Belmar Bay, which Sam also thinks could be the name of the show. It follows a teenage girl named Miley who moves there to live with her distant aunt after both her parents die. She meets a group of girls and goes to a bonfire with them and at midnight, it’s her seventeenth birthday, and she goes into the ocean and turns into a mermaid. She then finds out that there’s a colony of mermaids in town, and her aunt is one of them, and there’s a group of people trying to erase their kind.

annotations.comment: I love that we get to learn about the show. Miley = Cyrus
Maybe another word here (I read it like “distant cousin” which seemed odd)

paragraphs.body: That’s the pilot, at least, which is also the episode Sam is most confident in. From there, Miley and her aunt and her friends get into dramatic situations and have to fight the bad guys. Miley has one main love interest so far, but Sam plans to give her at least three. And probably a love triangle at some point.

annotations.comment: I really loved that Sam’s grasp of this show severely dwindles.
annotations.context_left: is also the episode Sam is most confident in. From there, Miley and her aunt and her friends get in
annotations.context_right: dramatic situations and have to fight the bad guys. Miley has one main love interest so far, but Sa

paragraphs.body: It’s this that she’s thinking about two miles into her run, the morning before she has to take useless Rob’s shift. The North Carolinian heat beats down onto her dad’s old UNC hat as Sam tries to brainstorm for the episode she started behind the counter the other day: one of Miley’s love interests just told her that his dad is the president of the club that’s trying to eradicate mermaids in town, and he doesn’t realize she’s one. Sam’s trying to decide if Miley should stop seeing him, or keep hiding her true self in order to get intel on the club’s plans.

Sam is leaning towards Miley hiding her identity when she reaches The Rock. At the end of her favorite running route, The Rock is a big boulder perched on the edge of a hillside looking right over the eleventh hole of Whitney Hope’s golf course. From the top of it, she has the perfect view to look down at the golfers and watch their toy-soldier-sized bodies move through the course.

They look like little video game characters from up there, and she likes to watch them play and imagine she’s their creator, their decisions and outcomes entirely up to her. She sometimes holds her pointer finger and thumb out into the air in front of her, squinting one eye as she hovers her hand over the players and imagines she can pluck them up and place them back wherever. She understands that this may be a little creepy, or concerning. Even Jess gave her a really strange look when she confessed this once.

annotations.comment: I enjoyed this. Nicely written.
annotations.context_left: n pluck them up and place them back wherever. She understands that this may be a little creepy, or c
annotations.context_right: rning. Even Jess gave her a really strange look when she confessed this once.

paragraphs.body: But from The Rock, looking out at the mountains in the distance, it reminds her that the world is more than just the country club and the gas station and the little yellow house.

She thinks of Naomi’s words again – how is she supposed to know how to get out of here? And when? And how? Her savings are not nearly enough to afford a car and multiple months of rent, not right now. So, does she wait? Or does she throw caution to the wind and just go?

annotations.comment: I don’t mind the indecision but it is a little confusing that she’s so sure what she wants to do and also doesn’t have a plan.

paragraphs.body: She wishes, for the millionth time in her life, that she had someone to talk to. A mom, or a dad, or a wise old aunt like Miley, or even an older sibling. Someone to show her the way, to guide her out of this town and onto better things.

annotations.comment: You might consider a brief thought about why she can’t/doesn’t talk to Jess.I loved that you connected what Miley has to what she wishes she had.

paragraphs.body: Out on the course, Sam watches a golf cart come whipping around a bend, the wheels making a screeching noise that even she could hear from her perch. She watches as a man with grey hair hops out from behind the wheel and storms towards one of the caddies. Sam can’t quite make out the words he’s saying, but she can tell that he’s chewing the guy out. The man is gesticulating wildly as he goes on, his hands pointing and flailing in the caddy’s face. The caddy is standing tall to him, though. Sam is surprised by this; no matter how many times customers yell at her, she still finds herself flinching and leaning away.

She had seen her father and Lynn act like this towards each other a few times. She didn’t remember him ever being so angry towards her mother, but she was also so young, so protected back then. And her mom’s sickness changed things; you couldn’t scream in the face of someone who could drop at any moment. It makes Sam wonder if, somehow, had they all known her dad’s fate, would Lynn have kept on screaming?

annotations.comment: Nice connection. I wondered if he ever screamed at her this way.

paragraphs.body: The berating on the course had stopped, and if Sam squinted, she could tell that the caddy had started speaking back. He was taller than the older man with a full head of dark brown hair mostly tucked under a cap.  His voice was contrastingly quiet, so much so that it felt eerie to hear almost nothing following the booming intensity of the man’s tirade. Sam was glued to the scene like it was a television show, wishing she had a microphone taped to something down there so she could hear the conversation. Whatever the caddy said, the man stared at him for a heated moment—Sam thought he might hit him—and let out one angry huff before getting back in his golf cart and zipping away.

annotations.comment: I really like this scene. It’s interesting. You’re smart to have a number of things happening.
Can she hear any snippets from either of them?

paragraphs.body: Sam released a breath she hadn’t realized she’d been holding. She found herself desperately wanting to know what the caddy had said to deescalate the situation and that angry man so effortlessly. It had been a while since Sam had been screamed at like that by anyone, but this caddy seemed to have some magic that she couldn’t help but crave.

annotations.comment: You might consider having this be a specific memory she has.

paragraphs.body: She dug her phone out of her waistband as her little characters continued, business as usual, on the course like nothing had ever happened. She should be starting her route home, she realized, especially if she wanted to have enough time to shower and write before her shift later. As she began running the miles back, she decided what Miley was going to do: she wasn’t going to run from the threat. She was going to stay quiet and fight.

annotations.comment: Like this connection. I just want to point out how the road trip/move isn’t really forefront in her mind here.

paragraphs.body: Sam arrived at her shift 5 minutes early in a terrible mood, and taking over from Dean only worsened it. If Rob was clueless, Dean was somehow even less coherent – what Jess would categorize as being a “brick” – and had managed to completely screw up the count for the cash register for the day so far. Sam all but shooed him out from behind the counter before using a wet wipe to quickly cleanse the space she would be spending the next 8 hours, then began counting what was currently in the cash register.

annotations.comment: Oh? Big change from where she was.

paragraphs.body: Her rotten mood had, unsurprisingly, come from an interaction with Lynn. When she got back from her run, Lynn was sitting at the miniature table in the kitchen. Sam realizes that the two of them haven’t sat there for a meal together since before her dad died; they either ate on the couch in front of the TV or in their separate bedrooms. So, Lynn sitting there with notebooks and random papers sprawled out before her was very odd. And slightly suspicious.

annotations.comment: Ah, okay.
Past tense?

paragraphs.body: “Are you doing payroll?” Sam asked as the wild screen door finished swinging behind her, clapping a few times against the door frame.

“No,” Lynn barely muttered out, her tongue sticking out of the corner of her mouth in a bizarre focus face. She’s wearing Sam’s dad’s old reading glasses, Sam realizes, and a zip of fire courses through her body.

annotations.comment: I really like this moment but would perhaps need to know a bit more about why this upsets her so.

paragraphs.body: “Are you going to do it soon?” Sam presses, “You owe me for like, three weeks of work.”
Lynn doesn’t respond for over thirty seconds, continuing to hover her pencil over the sheet of paper she’s staring at as if Sam had never said anything.

annotations.comment: This line should not be indented, but otherwise, I fixed this. Every new line of dialogue gets its own line.

paragraphs.body: “Hello?” Sam nudges.

Lynn throws her pencil down, and turns dramatically, “Sam. Leave me alone. Can’t you tell I’m doing something here? I mean, Jesus Christ,” she’s staring at Sam, who is fighting a laugh. Lynn must not have brushed her hair today, and her fake red hair with smudgy gray roots is sticking up in every which way. The reading glasses are making her eyes look huge and bug-like, magnifying the crusted mascara stuck to her eyelashes. It’s hard to take her seriously.

annotations.comment: Again, you’ve shifted into present tense here but the story is in the past.

paragraphs.body: “What are all those papers for then?”

“Go away,” Lynn locates her pencil and turns back to the pages that just look like numbers and scribbles to Sam, who steps closer to try and peer over Lynn’s shoulder. She notices, and swipes the papers into a messy pile, borderline screeching: “GO. I don’t ask what you’re doing in that tattered little notebook of yours, do I? Who raised you?”

Sam walks away, heading down the tiny galley kitchen to her bedroom. Lynn is still rambling to herself as she goes. As Sam showers and gets ready to go to work, she becomes increasingly disturbed: she has never once seen Lynn crunching numbers. Why start now?

annotations.comment: Oh, my stomach clenched here, too.

paragraphs.body: On her way out of the house, Lynn is still sitting at the table. “Hey,” she calls out as Sam tries to leave without a word, “Take this.” She’s holding out a rectangular bundle covered in brown paper and wrapped so many times with clear packing tape that it seems like it would be a prank to give this to anyone. “A guy named Benjamin will be stopping by the station later. Give that to him, will you?”

annotations.comment: Yikes, Lynn is really coming off as worrying.

paragraphs.body: Lynn’s favors usually looked like this: giving something strange to someone, or taking something strange to give to her. Sam tried to pry and find out what she was doing: she’s had theories of drug dealing or prostitution or tax evasion. Jess is still convinced she’s selling drugs, but Sam isn’t so sure. The items are different every time; sometimes a package the size of her hand, sometimes a slim envelope, sometimes a box as big as a chihuahua. She made the mistake of asking Lynn what was in the packages one too many times, and they’ve been intensely wrapped ever since. Sam decided that ignorance was bliss, and whatever Lynn is up to must not be too bad if it can be handled in just a few little packages every once and awhile.

annotations.comment: I had a little trouble believing that a girl as smart as Sam wouldn’t be much more reluctant to do this.

paragraphs.body: The favors have slowed down in the past few months; this is the first one Sam has been asked to do in weeks. Lynn always does it around the time a paycheck is due, hanging it over Sam’s head. So, Sam yanks the package out of Lynn’s hand and tucks it into the tote bag hanging from her shoulder, saying “I want my check when I come back” and turning on her heel.

annotations.comment: Maybe make this more explicit? Tie the favor to the paycheck?

paragraphs.body: “Put your name tag on!” Lynn calls after her, the screen door slamming loudly in response.

annotations.comment: Let this belong to Sam.

paragraphs.body: Behind the counter, Sam had only made it to the dimes before the bell over the door rang and her first customer came in. Or rather customers, she realizes with a tightening chest as she watches the grey-haired man from the golf course walk in, trailed a few steps by the dark-haired caddy. The caddy, she realizes now, is Jack Barrett, her former classmate. She sat next to him at graduation. Jack Barrett, Sam Bates. He played baseball and they had geometry together sophomore year. She doesn’t know much about him beyond that.

annotations.comment: I’m a little surprised that she didn’t recognize someone she knows by sight so well.

paragraphs.body: She greets them with a hello, then pretends to not watch them as they go through the aisles. She doesn’t know what she’s expecting, but she’s on edge, nonetheless, almost waiting for another blowout argument to ensue. They seem to be calm though, casually browsing without a word to each other or Sam. The store is silent, aside from the constant buzz from the coolers and the dripping soda machine.

When they make their way up to the counter, it seems that they’ve just been collecting various snacks. The grey-haired man sets down two Diet Cokes and a pack of Altoids, and Jack adds a blue Powerade and a bag of chips to the pile.

“And I need 30 dollars on pump 1,” the man says, pulling his wallet out of his pocket as Sam starts ringing up their items. His voice is as gruff and affronting as she would expect after his scene earlier. She nods, and as she enters that into the system the man’s phone begins to ring in his pocket. He hands Jack a fifty-dollar bill and leaves the store.
“Your total is $43.89,” Sam tells him.

“Okay,” Jack replies, glancing at her and then turning to look through the glass doors that face out into the lot. The man is out there, his back turned as the gas pumps into his shiny car while he talks on the phone. Jack takes the chance to duck into an aisle, grabbing a candy bar and asking, “Can I get two scratch-offs, too?”

annotations.comment: Maybe some acknowledgment between them?

paragraphs.body: “Sure, I just need to see your ID quickly.”
He nods, reaching into his own pocket and handing her his license. He turned 18 in February. When Sam looks back up at him, Jack is staring at her with a slightly furrowed brow.
“Do you just want the dollar ones?” Sam asks, slipping off her stool and reaching for the rolls of lottery tickets.
“Yeah, thanks,” there’s a beat of silence as Sam tears off two Dollar Dash’s, “Did you go to Briarwood?” Jack asks. He recognizes her too, then.

annotations.comment: Hmm, maybe he needs to be even further from her memory? I know I’m harping on this but I’m just having a little trouble with the concept. But another way to think about it is, where is this section going? It feels a little… slow.

paragraphs.body: “Yep. Your total is $47.15 now,” she says. He hands her the fifty, and once she drops the change into his hand he pours it into the nearly empty tip bucket that Dean and Rob insist on keeping out on the counter. Sam usually puts it away at the beginning of her shift, but had forgotten in the midst of Dean’s change screwup.
Sam shifts her gaze from Jack’s hand to his face, offering a tight-lipped smile: “Thanks.”

annotations.comment: Love this moment — he is doing something genuinely nice and yet she hates it.

paragraphs.body: He nods, grabbing his bag and heading to the door, “Have a good one.”

The bell chimes, and Sam is once again alone in the QuikGo. She leans a little over the counter, craning her neck to watch Jack climb in the passenger seat before the grey-haired man zips away. She’s left with so many questions, she doesn’t even know which one she would ask first if she got the chance. For a moment, she wants so desperately to know their dynamic, to know how they can shift from chaos to calm, to know what else goes on when she’s not there to observe. But then she remembers that she can’t know. That she shouldn’t. She has twenty days until all ties can be cut; now is not the time to start wondering about the regulars. Instead, she goes back to counting the change in the register.

annotations.text: She’s left with so many questions,
annotations.comment: I honestly don’t quite follow what these questions are yet. Can you let us into her thoughts a bit? She might even ask him something: “Is that your dad?”
I didn’t quite buy that she suddenly remembers her leaving here, instead of when she’s annoyed with Lynn (or perhaps slightly scared of her).

paragraphs.body: Sam’s next few days are mind-numbingly boring. She spends her time doing one of four things: sleeping, running, working, or writing. She’s been trying to talk to Jess, but she’s been busier than usual and can hardly answer texts, much less phone calls. Sam is behind the counter at the QuikGo on Wednesday when she hears back from Jess for the first time in days. Most recently, Sam sent a link to a one-bedroom apartment in Brooklyn:
Jess: hey sorry, I’ve been rlly busy. that apt is cool

Sam reads it three times, and then gives it a few minutes, waiting to see if Jess has more to add and is just taking her time writing it out. But there’s nothing else from her, and Sam is more than a little disturbed. This isn’t the Jess she knows, who has never used a comma in text messages in her life. Sam scrolls back in her mind, trying to think if she could’ve possibly done something wrong to upset Jess – she comes up blank. Their last phone call ended positively, and they’ve texted so infrequently in the days since that it would be impossible for her to have stirred up a silent treatment. Something must be up.

annotations.comment: Love this moment of trying to parse someone’s texts. Feels very current.

Again, like this. I don’t think you need the several days gap, if they’re just boring days. Why can’t the text arrive later that same afternoon?Charlotte, your work remains strong and intriguing. I’m really excited to see where it goes. I love Sam and am so interested in her. It’s fun to see her world through her eyes — I get a very good sense of these people and can tell Sam is actually a pretty good writer. I will say, the story remains pretty slow. I get the sense the author is still looking for the story’s events, a bit. You may have to allow yourself to be a little less polished at a sentence level so that the story can get going on a manuscript level. Good luck and happy writing!
